content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Bugonia – worth a watch

**Score:** 157
**Body:** Just watched Bugonia and honestly didn’t expect much but it turned out to be really good. Definitely worth a watch.

Emma Stone was great in this, felt very natural. Also read that she actually shaved her head for the role, which is pretty wild and adds to the character. Jesse Plemons was really good too, solid performance from him.

What I liked most is that it doesn’t drag. It kind of quietly keeps you hooked the whole time, no unnecessary scenes or filler. And the twist towards the end was actually solid, didn’t see it coming.

Overall, a really pleasant surprise for me. Would recommend 👍

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I see absolutely no reason why Jesse Plemons was not nominated for best actor for his role in this movie. He did phenomenal.
  - Not only nominated but should've won. 
2. Why would you not expect much from Yorgos?
3. Talking about "the twist towards the end" is a spoiler. Think about it.
  - I second this. Anyone saying “twist” ruins a movies surprise. 
4. Ok, i'm sold, putting it on now! Thanks 😊 
5. The original is worth a watch if you enjoyed the remake.  
6. I loved that movie and then watch the director’s entire collection. Top notch crazy in the best way.
7. Lanthimos is a great director, but in this case watch the Korean original movie. It’s way more fun. 

Save the Green Planet! - 2003
8. This movie was one of the best I've seen of 2025, and I watched about 75 movies in 2025

## 2. Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die, is such an underrated movie. It's one of the best movies I've seen in a long while for what I thought was a joke movie.

**Score:** 50
**Body:** Honestly an incredible movie from the start to the plot twist at the end. Loved it. Not for everyone but a definite shame for how much it flopped.





### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I loved this movie. I really wish it was shown more nationally.
2. I saw it as well and thoroughly enjoyed it.
3. Fantastic movie with fantastic twists. I’ve been telling everyone about it 
4. Quick question. Would someone who loved Everything Everywhere All At Once find similar aspects in this movie to love? I ask because from what i’ve seen/heard it kind of gives me that vibe. I’ll probably see it regardless because I really like Sam Rockwell but just wanted to know
  - I loved EEAAO and I found Good Luck to be a rather soulless attempt at recapturing the spirit. It had decently high highs, but the lows were very boring and ultimately created a movie I found very stupid for the sake of being stupid. Always worth seeing Rockwell but it was a 2 1/2 out of 5 for me, where EEAAO is a clear 5/5
    - Ok i really appreciate your input. I’ll watch it but not with Everything Everywhere in mind, just as it’s own thing and hopefully it’s at least a decent group watch. I know that i really liked the director’s last movie “A Cure for Wellness”
5. I loved it. 
6. This movie totally had me until the last 20 mins which I still enjoyed but I just didn’t like it as much as I had until that point. It’s also what stopped me from recommending it to others. 
7. Another great Sam Rockwell movie! He kinda does all the heavy lifting of the movie, so if you're not a Rockwell fan, then you may not enjoy it as much.
8. I really wanted to go see it but it was only showing at my theater for like 3 days… 

## 3. The World's Fastest Indian (2005)

**Score:** 34
**Body:** This was an incredibly uplifting, feel-good film that left me grinning from ear to ear. 

Based on a true story, the film follows Burt Munro, an eccentric and eternally optimistic New Zealander who spends decades tinkering with a 1920 Indian motorcycle in his humble backyard shed. He has one goal in life: to ship his makeshift, cobbled-together motorcycle all the way to the legendary Bonneville Salt Flats in Utah and test how fast it can truly go.

The character of Burt Munro is a spiritual kin to Rudy (1993), so if you enjoy a good underdog story about the dogged human spirit then definitely give this one a watch.

The film is genre-defying and could be described as a racing film + a road trip movie + the ultimate underdog story.

Runtime: 2h 7m

P.S.- Sir Anthony Hopkins steps into the shoes of Burt Munro and delivers one of the warmest, most lovable, and genuinely charming performances of his entire career.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's a wonderful movie
2. I love this movie. It also has Walton Goggins! I really enjoyed that there’s no enemy in it. Pretty much every character is trying to help him reach his goal in one way shape or form. 
3. saw it in the theater. 

saw the shit out of it. 
4. So before I did a month trip to NZ we were given a list of movies about Kiwis and this one was on the list. Really enjoyed it but I did go ‘don’t think this really told me much about the people of NZ…but happy anyways’
  - Genuinely feel the LOTR trilogy would've been more helpful 
    - They actually specifically told us to avoid it.   Basically our bus drove by dozens of the locations, 

we stopped at the foot of Mount Doom, it was the first time a lot of our group had ever seen snow

We went to Hobbiton post reconstruction.  Before it was redone for The hobbit

We went over a bridge that went over the watering hole where Sméagol first found the ring at the beginning of ROTK.   

And last but not least, someone bought the trilogy so we actually watched it on the bus during our night drives.
      - That sounds so frickin cool. A bucket list item for sure. 
        - Yea. It wasn’t a LOtR focused trip but they knew what the college kids wanted. we had a free day in Wellington and a museum there had a HUGE LOTR exhibit that I spent hours in.



## 4. Films with amazing scores that don't get talked about enough

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I'm looking for movies that have beautiful music scores that you think are underrated/doesn't get talked about enough.

AKA something that you rarely or never see praised on the internet. So no movies like Interstellar or Tenet or Lord of the rings. Stuff like that.

If you can, please try to refrain from repeating movies already mentioned in the comments and as always, thank you for everyone that comments!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Last of the Mohicans
2. Maybe I’m just missing it, but Nick Cave and Warren Ellis’s score for The Assassination of Jesse James. I listen to some tracks on that pretty often. 

They also did The Proposition and Hell or High Water. 
3. Sorcerer and Thief by Tangerine Dream
4. I never see the “Gravity” score mentioned, it’s phenomenal 
5. Once upon a time in the west
6. The Fountain OST. Clint Mansell and the Kronos Quartet. Incredible. 
7. Batman Returns (1992) - Danny Elfman

Ghost in the Shell (1995) - Kenji Kawai

Good Will Hunting (1997) - Danny Elfman

Kikujiro (1999) - Joe Hisaishi
8. Conan the Barbarian, original score by Basil Poledouris.


9. Some of my favorites:

A Little Princess - Patrick Doyle
The Secret Garden - Zbigniew Preisner
Brick - Nathan Johnson
The Boy and the Heron - Joe Hisaishi
Coraline - Bruno Coulais
Emma. - Isobel Waller-Bridge
Hero - Tan Dun
Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon - Tan Dun
Amelie - Yann Tiersen
10. Legends of the Fall

Little Women (1994)

## 5. movies that are epic sagas

**Score:** 20
**Body:** i love movies that are epic in scale and show the journey/transformation of characters. for example:The Godfather,Heat,Goodfellas and and Dhurandhar.

its always a cherry on top when the films are long.

would love some suggestions!!!!!!!!!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lawrence Of Arabia
2. Once Upon A Time In America (1984)
3. The Seven Samurai
4. Kingdom of Heaven Director’s Cut
5. The Good The Bad & The Ugly 
6. Malcolm X 


Gandhi


Heaven's Gate


Lawrence of Arabia


Doctor Zhivago 


Napoleon Directors Cut










7. I'm gonna say the obvious because nobody else mentioned it yet: The Lord of the Rings movies

  
Other than that maybe Seven Samurai or Ben Hur
8. Little Big Man

Jeremiah Johnson

Dances With Wolves

How the West Was Won
9. Gangs of New York
10. The 13th Warrior

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Jackie Earle Haley in Watchmen delivers one of the best performances of the 21st century

**Score:** 1975
**Body:** Rewatched Watchmen recently and my goodness…

Haley absolutely blew me away with his performance as Rorschach. He stole every scene he was in. He made the movie for me! 

Everytime he speaks, you can feel every emotion. Rage, conviction, sadness, etc. Not to mention he’s an absolute badass.

The interrogation scene with the psychiatrist and his confrontation with Dr. Manhattan are my favorite scenes. Him screaming “DO IT!!!” never fails to give me chills. 

He deserved an oscar nomination for this. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “…you’re locked in here with ME!”
  - Just the unbridled rage, madness, hellfire erupting, he’s in the shit but he’s giving it all right back and then some.
  - Channel 4 (UK) cut this really weirdly for the ad break. He screams 'You're locked in here with m' (ad break) 'eee'.
    - Ha. No way. I remember that. 
  - My wife hates when I use that scene to describe our marriage .
2. Agreed. To this day, The Ultimate Cut of Watchmen is one of my favorite comic book adaptations. 
3. I remember seeing the Bad News Bears, back in the 70s when I was a kid. He was the bad boy rebel in that movie and we all thought he was great in it. I didn't see him for a while, and then he popped up in some good movies like Watchmen. I'm glad his career is doing good. He was definitely a bad boy rebel in Watchmen, as well.
  - “Never compromise. Not even in the face of Armageddon.”

Man that gives me chills! I agree with you, Haley is a phenomenal actor.
4. I agree. He was the best part of the movie. Him and the opening credits. 
5. The accumulated filth of all their sex and murder will foam up about their waists and all the whores and politicians will look up and shout "Save us!"

And I'll whisper...

"No.."

## 2. What’s a movie where the whole cast is superb?

**Score:** 1239
**Body:** Just watched One Flew Over the Cuckoo’s Nest for the first time. Oh my god now I understand why people say this is one of the best movies of all time. Every actor here is EXCEPTIONAL. This movies proves that Nicholson is one of the best actors of all time. 

What’s a movie where the whole cast is exceptional? 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mars Attacks. Even the aliens were superb
2. Glengarry Glen Ross
3. ![gif](giphy|fdP6TS6bhzij6)


4. Heat
5. The Departed.
  - It's funny, this and my choice (Boogie Nights) are the only two Wahlberg films where he did a legit good job, IMHO.

And in the Departed, he was just channeling his old Dorchester self, so...
    - Respectfully I think he was pretty funny in The Other Guys
6. 12 angry men - 
Snatch - 
Ocean's Eleven
7. The Princess Bride. I don't honestly think you could put together as good a cast today if some lunatic studio exec decided to try and remake it.
8. The Godfather

## 3. If you could give an Oscar to someone, who would it be?

**Score:** 842
**Body:** Can be anyone: actor, director, producer. 

For me, William Dafoe. Pretty self-explanatory. He should have at least 2 by now. My close second would be Ralph Fiennes. Also great actor. 

What’s your pick? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|2zGdmOE2CLivK3gKz8|downsized)
2. ![gif](giphy|RSnbPf5osSUKMdzwog|downsized)

Shame he doesn’t have one already
3. Jesse plemons
4. John c. Reilly. Actually an incredible actor with range from step bros to gangs of new york and thin red line. Guy is just a good actor who has a ton of fun with his roles and makes them his own
  - Boogie Nights and Chicago too. How has he not won an Oscar yet?
5. ![gif](giphy|HgtCxKvJZ7Wi4)
6. ![gif](giphy|Dvsoh4iw8ddpC)

Sir Ian McKellen, I feel as though he should have won best supporting actor for The Fellowship of the Ring.
7. This guy...

https://preview.redd.it/jw9i76xemkqg1.jpeg?width=428&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=99cf21493fe17f6e0472a7904fd6e65e80bb8057
8. Steve Buscemi
9. ![gif](giphy|3o7TKvuOivTa8LWiDC|downsized)

## 4. Project Hail Mary 11/10

**Score:** 704
**Body:** Okay so this is in definitely going to be in my top 5 of the year. I highly doubt it will leave the top spot. Project Hail Mary is a visually stunning and extremely touching film. I loved the friendship between Grace and Rocky. There was a lot of humor. Ryan Gosling's performance is even better than his performance in Drive. Seriously I think next awards season he's going to sweep. This movie is a absolute must on the big screen. Unfortunately my nearest theater doesn't have anything like IMAX so just saw standard but I can imagine the experience being even better. It didn't feel like 2 hours and 36 minutes. I was engaged from beginning to end. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I saw the 830 showing this morning in IMAX and it was great! My gf and I didn’t even feel like the movie dragged on or felt bored. We both felt fully engaged for the entire duration. Gosling’s performance felt down to earth and convincing. Nothing cringe about it. Real good movie all around: I don’t have anything negative to say at the moment. Still thinking about it a few hours later. Solid movie! And first movie seen on the big screen this year! It was supposed to be Undertone but no one wanted to go with me lol. I guess that’ll have to wait for streaming.
tldr : Project Hail Mary gets 2 thumbs down! 👎🏽👎🏽
(If you’ve seen the movie, you’ll get it)
2. I adore this movie

![gif](giphy|9EvnXdZaUZbCqScn67)
  - https://i.redd.it/f2pwj790onqg1.gif
    - I want Funko to make a little rocky figure
  - Fist my bump.
  - Commenting on Project Hail Mary 11/10...

https://i.redd.it/eh68pgwvnnqg1.gif
3. Amazing movie, so damn good 
4. Me who thought this movie was just ok recommending it to others:

https://preview.redd.it/6njb6o45unqg1.jpeg?width=1080&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=867d1d11f19b291cc5acd38fa47b4947c856d084
5. Just came out of the IMAX screening and l love this. It was fun, exciting, cute, sometimes sad. I thought i was going in with expectations that were sky high but boy did it meet and exceed them
6. Im seeing it on Wednesday, the book was utterly amazing!

## 5. Name Underrated movies everyone should watch at least once

**Score:** 613

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Putting two images of *A Scanner Darkly* is the most A Scanner Darkly thing you could have done.
  - Im flipping back and forth looking for the subtle difference...
2. Dark City (1998)
  - Warning to those who haven’t seen it; only watch the Director’s Cut. Trust me. 
3. Swiss Army Man

Edit: definitely less known but one of my all time favourite animated films "Son of the White Mare" (1981)
4. Pandorum. I don’t know why it’s so overlooked. It’s a good film with good acting and some twists of the story. 
5. Cube (1997)
6. Blue Valentine (2009) starring Ryan Gosling and Michelle Williams 

An aching portrayal of a falling marriage 
7. Lucky Number Slevin. 
8. Pull a double feature with Waking Life.

Most of Linklater’s catalogue is under rated.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Why can’t people just sit and watch a movie anymore?

**Score:** 85
**Body:** I swear I’m not trying to sound like an old man here but what happened to just… sitting down, putting your phone away, and actually watching a movie all the way through

I feel like every time I watch something with other people now there’s constant distractions, phones out, pausing, talking, checking something, rewinding because someone missed a scene, and it completely kills the experience. Even when I’m alone I catch myself reaching for my phone during slower moments which I never used to do

Movies are literally designed to pull you in for a couple hours, build tension, pacing, atmosphere, all of that, and it just doesn’t land the same if you’re half paying attention

Is this just how it is now or am I just noticing it more? Do people still actually sit down and watch movies without multitasking or is that kind of gone at this point

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Movies used to be special. There was never a time where you had screens in your hand that could do anything else ever, so when you watched a movie it was easier to have your full attention. These days they’re less special because we can literally watch a movie anywhere. 

I still watch movies with undivided attention. And lots of people still do. I generally don’t see screens out in theaters and stuff, occasionally there are people who are doing other stuff but for the most part they’re engaged. Maybe you’re also just watching movies with people who don’t care for that particular movie. 
2. You’ve answered your own question. It’s the phones and the way we consume media today. We’re addicted to a constant flow of little dopamine hits. Slow parts of a movie aren’t fulfilling your addiction so you reach for the phone. 
3. One of the reasons i go to the theater is I need the social pressure to keep me off my phone. If i see other people on their phone for more than a quick check I'll ask nicely to put their phone away. During the charli xcx movie a guy put his phone up to start taking a video of the screen (his bright phone in the middle of my view of the screen) it wasn't a request. "Put your phone AWAY." He jumped, looked embarrassed, and put it away immediately.


In some ways we do gotta reinforce the social contract. Not starting fights but just let people know other people can see and are affected by their behavior. 
4. What surprises me (at my local theater at least) is how much *more* quiet the audiences are today vs 30-40 years ago when I was a kid and people used to talk all the time. Perhaps because these days I go to Thursday night previews which sell out quickly and the audience is already filtered into mostly die hard film buffs, but in general my local theater (Century City AMC in Los Angeles) behaves well.

What you may not know is how damn loud and rowdy audiences were earlier in film history. Remember that films were originally silent so it wasn't a big deal to talk during the show.

In the 1940s and 1950s, movie theaters were a great place to hang out for the day to get out of the weather (with many theaters in the south in the US being some of the first commercial buildings with air conditioning) and when you bought a ticket, you just stayed there all day. Watch the news, movie or two, come in during the middle of the film or even the end - didn't matter. I remember going to the theater with my grandfather when I was little and he didn't understand why we had to get up and leave at the end of the film. In fact his first job was as a lobby attendant at a movie theater in California where he had a flashlight and showed people to their seats back in the 1930s.

And before that you'd probably be horrified to sit in an opera box in the late 19th century. Audiences were so loud that performers would have to stop the show to beg the audience to shut up. And in some cases, the actors were literally chased off stage by angry audience members.

Before that, in Shakespeare's time, same deal - you would be shocked how quiet the actual performances were and how loud the audiences were. Think like a modern day baseball game with people walking up and down the aisles selling drinks and snacks.

Before that, Ancient Greek theater was the same deal.

Point is, audiences sitting quietly during a performance are a relatively new thing.
  - The criminally overlooked comedy MATINEE (1993) with John Goodman as a William Castle like promoter is a great reference for what it used to be like as a kid on Saturday afternoon 

Obviously it’s a little over the top but not that much especially on a horror film marketed to younger audiences 
5. Honestly I don’t even think it’s movies.
I think we just lost the ability to sit with something that doesn’t instantly reward us. Like the second it slows down my brain goes “phone?” and I hate that I do it.
6. It's everyone now.

The phenomenon is called Second Screen viewing.  Netflix is rumored to producesl many of its original movies while fully aware that you'll probably be more interested in your phone than the film. So they allegedly make movies with this in mind.

https://www.theguardian.com/tv-and-radio/2025/jan/17/not-second-screen-enough-is-netflix-deliberately-dumbing-down-tv-so-people-can-watch-while-scrolling
7. If I really want to watch a movie, I’ll watch it by myself. If it’s something I’m less interested in, I’ll watch it with my girlfriend. I need to be fully immersed but she likes talking during movies and looking at her phone.
8. I'm in the same boat as you when watching alone. The last few weeks I have started leaving my phone in another room. It's made a world of difference. I have no urge to reach for it because it's not there. There is no buzz that makes me think I'm just going to see if it's an important message before I'm somehow browsing eBay. If you're able to do it, give it a try.
9. I have really bad inattentive adhd and I unfortunately use my phone way too much, but I can focus on movies just fine. I’d recommend leaving your phone in another room or keeping it on silent. I always do that and I’m never tempted to check

## 2. Wuthering Heights is the Cheapest Type of Film

**Score:** 67
**Body:** I’m very curious to hear the sentiment and see if this statement is too strong or overly critical, but I left Wuthering Heights significantly disappointed. Being a fan of the novel, I obviously don’t expect adaptations to be exactly the same or criticise a director for having their own interpretation, even if that interpretation is significantly differently. Although, how could a director adapt one of the greatest novels ever written and turn such material into cheap exploitation? Fennell’s efforts in using the cheapest form of shock value to immerse the audience just seemed so distasteful and exaggerated that it borderline parody. I mean, the film was trying so forcefully to be ‘sexy’ that it almost proved comically. At the point where Isabella is chained up like a dog, I started actively disliking the film and I think I lost some respect for the director. I liked Promising Young Women, but this was just such an exploitative effort at shock value. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I thought the film was actually less shocking and more tame than what I was expecting. I didn’t know much about the novel, but this film is probably the least shocking, to me, of Fennell’s films. Saltburn really went for it and in comparison Wurthering Heights is fairly tame. I wanted more grossness and sexuality, but found the lack of that to actually be a tease, much like the relationship between Heathcliff and Cathy, lacking satisfaction.

I enjoy Fennell’s visual style and I would go see anything of her’s just for the design and composition she puts on the screen. I think her style is downplayed because folks don’t like her characters and their choices.
2. I think that's exactly the movie we should expect from the writer-director who gave us Promising Young Woman and Saltburn: a narrative that's basically about obsession. It seemed to me that Fennel saw Heathcliff and Cathy as the 'ultimate toxic couple' in the popular imaginary and just ran with is. It's obviously a superficial take when we take into account the deeper themes in the novel, but I personally enjoy how she reframed Isabella as someone who could get some kicks from submission, and how a sub also holds some sort of power in a particular dynamic: sometimes, there can't be a 'dom' without a 'sub' to consent. 
3. For a film you characterize as using cheap exploitation for shock value, it is missing most of the genre elements we attribute to  exploitation films. There's no nudity - nary a nipple in sight. The gore and violence is minimal. The visuals that people find shocking or gross are usually allegorical (eggs, fish, walls, etc.) and not the actual sex in the movie. This isn't really how exploitation cinema operates as a genre.

You can find it shocking or in bad taste - anyone is entitled to their opinion on that score. But that's not the same thing as it being cheap exploitation.
4. I love thrashy exploitative films with lots of ridiculous style and this movie delivered for me. Is it absolutely absurd? Of course. Is it a good adaptation of the magnificent novel? Of course not.

I think Emerald Fennell has a strong voice as a director and will continue to make her mark, for better or worse.

I'll take the wacky stylistic swings and absurd exploitation above stuff like The Grey Man any day, which for me is the most useless type of mediocrity out there. Wuthering Heights crackles with real energy and personality, even if that energy and personality is understandably off putting for a lot of the audience, especially those who are there to see a slavish adaptation.

I would recommend it to those who are fans of both Douglas Sirk and Baz Luhrmann, or even Bram Stoker's Dracula. Questionable taste and a lot of chutzpah is my bag.
  - Here’s the thing for me and this film: I love period films, I love when directors also have fun with period films or classic novels.

And yet- to me it didn’t feel like Emerald stylistically adapted a film, it felt like  she outed herself as entirely missing the source material without understanding it, even ironically.

Maybe she meant to Jane Eyre and forgot but Wuthering Heights is a specific kind of story, a gothic tale of obsession to the point of being totally unhealthy to the point of it following after death.

Emerald actually ignores the books juiciest elements and opts to focus on parts that are so surface I’m not even kidding when I say it feels like she skimmed the cliff notes.

Like there IS. a version of Wuthering Heights that could be dripping with sex, and longing and heavy amounts of style but if wouldn’t be this.

Instead turn after turn I could just hear Emeralds voice going “eh we don’t need that” like Heathcliffe being a man of objectively a different race and that being a factor.

Or the slow onset of psychosis to the plot of seeing ghosts to which Emerald says “Ohh ghosts? That’s weird, let’s lose that and end the book early”

In short this wasn’t a stylistic adaptation it was a failure to capture any memorable aspect of the book which is a shame because there very much could be one, it just wasn’t this.



  - Yeah, I guess it depends on perspective and taste. I actually found it lacked energy because it’s crackling was being produced in the most easiest manner possible. I found the route in which it achieved its energy a cop-out and a terrible disservice to the source material. 
5. Adaptations exists for many reasons. Some directors want a crack as slavishly adapting the material and others want to put their own stamp on it.  Emerald Fennell has said about her approach:

>The first thing was, because I love the book so much and because I can appreciate how gigantic it is and how original and how personal it is, I had to learn to understand that making a movie adaptation of it could never really exactly mirror the experience of reading the book. And therefore, I had to look to myself and my experience. \[The film\] was about building a world that was one reader’s, one girl’s, interpretation of reading the book—which has certain wish fulfillment in there, things that happened that I wished would happen but maybe didn’t, a certain amount of grace extended, and then withheld, too. It’s so much a movie that, I hope, kneels at the shrine of Emily Brontë, but also one that is an acknowledgement of what it is to feel somebody’s work very personally.

It is conforming to how she internalized the book. Surely everyone who has read the work or seen previous adaptations have all viewed it from their personal lens and it's fair that Fennell does the same. 
  - [deleted]
  - Perhaps the way she internalised it reveals her to be shallow and vapid.

## 3. What are your thoughts on the rapid, jump-cut editing style seen in Project Hail Mary?

**Score:** 42
**Body:** It has two key features:

1) Very rapid cutting that never slows down, Tony Scott style. Even in slower dialogue scenes the editing stays very fast. Why show someone walking down a hall in one standard shot when you can do it in four shots from odd, beautiful angles? 

2) Jump cuts galore, Youtube or music video style. I've never seen a fictional film with so many jump cuts. A scene that in other films would take place in 30 seconds occurs in Project Hail Mary as a 3 second shot - jump forward in time - a two second shot - jump forward in time - a three second shot - scene over. 

This style has the advantage of being youthful and exciting, keeping the audience's attention captivated, and moving a dense and "sciencey" story along quickly. But it has the potential disadvantage of being too annoyingly  fast and frantic for some people, making the action of some scenes unclear, and not giving the audience enough time to respond properly to the reality or emotion of some scenes.

What is your opinion of the editing style?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't know for sure but i think it's a tactical choice based on a few things. First, this is one of those movies that is trying to cram in all the high points of a book. I think they filmed a lot of those extra parts and scenes and dialogue but getting the movie tight required a lot of cuts. 

Relatedly, they are also trying to keep things moving quickly while also communicating to the audience that a lot more time is passing than you are being shown.

 Finally, they are working with an elaborate puppet. The cuts probably help with the illusion and limit the amount of CGI needed. 

  - I found the style jarring at first. That scenario seems like an invitation for a classic slow-burn opening where you're gradually introduced to the character and the situation, right?

But when I realized that they weren't going to cut ANY major plot points I felt it was the right move. The movie was like a stone skipping across a lake of humor. If it slowed down it would sink.
  - And they still landed at over 2.5 hours
    - I didn't feel that length at all.  I didn't even bother to look up the runtime beforehand, and when I walked out at the end I was surprised how late it was.
  - I heard Lord & Miller say (from memory) that they did 11 test screenings and they noted any scenes where the audience appeared to be bored and revised them. Maybe that's another reason why every scene is edited so fast.
    - A surefire way to end up with trash pacing, how sad. Every frame a painting has a very good video essay on this exact topic:


https://youtu.be/3Q3eITC01Fg
2. I haven't seen the film, but these are the directors of the 21 Jump Street movies, right?  I think their style is just to shoot too much footage and then let the editor figure it out later.

3. It was effective in that it reflected Ryland’s thought process, especially in the beginning. I mean, dude woke up after a coma spanning multiple years, inside a spaceship with the best lab equipment Earth has to offer, and an SSD with all the information on Earth, every scientific paper, every IP, etc. 

He was a kid in a candy shop, and all this information was hitting him like a truck, it’d be difficult to capture that feeling with longer shots

The editing was adjusted well enough at the more emotional moments, like >!Rocky offering his astrophage and their not really last goodbye!< so I’m not mad
  - yeah it was only really ‘unpleasantly’ fast editing at the beginning, and that’s when he has absolutely no idea what’s happening or what he’s doing so it fits quite well

## 4. Noble feelings of love

**Score:** 19
**Body:** There is a kind of love that sometimes brings tears to my eyes when I think about it (I'm quite an emotional person sometimes). When you love someone so much that you want them to get stronger. When someone loves me, I feel so happy, it's natural, I'd enjoy it a lot. If I hear someone tell me "I can't live without you", that would fill my heart with joy.

Yet... there is some kind of feeling that's way beyond that. A kind of feeling that's so noble and pure that it feels beyond my reach. To love someone so much that you want them to be capable of living without you. You'd help them not to be too attached to you, and it pains you, because you're human, you like being loved. Yet you choose that pain.

It's a mesmerizing kind of love.

Here are some of the movies I like that capture some aspects of family love:

* **The Flavor of Green Tea Over Rice**
* **The Straight Story** how sometimes family members drift apart over things that don't really matter, and how it's never too late
* **Hell or High Water**, a movie about brotherhood
* **My Life (1993)**: one of my favorite and impactful movies on my life, it changed my relationship with parents.
* **My Neighbors the Yamadas:** what it means to be a family, I will never forget the umbrella scene.
* **Late Spring**: to love someone so much that you help them make a decision, that will make you lonely.
* **Tokyo Godfathers**

What do you think? do you know any nice movies that carry this feeling?

4. In The Mood For Love is the one that expresses that kind of love to me. Heartbreaking and visually beautiful. And I would wear every single dress that Su wears in the movie. 

I have my own story like that. A much younger man fell in love with me, but I ended it even though I loved him too, because I didn’t want to hold him back from finding someone his age and starting a family. Or have him still being relatively young and having to care for me as I age. 
  - I love sincere feelings in movies and stories, but, when you see it in front of you, real decisions made by a real person, it hits different. and perhaps I cry when I heard such stories, because... I wonder if I were in that position... what would I do?  
I wish you all the happiness in your life.
5. That’s a beautiful way of putting it, love as letting go, not holding on. If that’s the feeling you’re chasing, Ikiru captures it in a quiet, devastating way, living for others without needing recognition. Still Walking also sits in that space of unspoken family love, where care exists alongside distance and regret. And for something that really embodies loving someone enough to let them go, Call Me by Your Name has that rare tenderness where love isn’t about possession, but growth, even if it hurts.

## 5. 9 (2009) is not underrated or underappreciated

**Score:** 12
**Body:** I've read a few takes that this animated movie is underrated or underappreciated. Having watched it, I disagree. It is appreciated in exactly the correct amount, being quite forgotten and largely unimpactful. It is not good cinema. A less-than-featurelength runtime for a theatrical feature is one red flag that may indicate pacing issues before having watched the film, but it's not the biggest issue here. That would be the fact that it is very difficult to care for the characters. There is non-stop action which is nevertheless stakeless and pointless. Overall, worth only 1 watch (being very generous here).
